metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief description in the What section of what rebar-implement's SKILL.md covers, specifically calling out that its review-and-land steps should reference project docs (rebar/Gerrit) rather than hardcoding a workflow.
findings.finding: AC #3 verifies a specific internal content property of rebar-implement ('review-and-land steps defer to project docs') that is never described in the plan body — the What section names rebar-implement as a skill directory with a SKILL.md but gives no description of its internal content or structure. This leaves the AC measuring something with no corresponding described deliverable.
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, expand the rebar-implement entry to something like: 'rebar-implement — guides an agent through planning, coding, and landing a change; review-and-land steps explicitly defer to the project's own docs (rebar plan + Gerrit workflow) rather than prescribing a fixed process.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define what 'supporting reference files' means for each skill (e.g., state a default like 'one REFERENCE.md per skill listing key commands/links, or none if not needed') so an agent can act without clarifying.
findings.finding: The plan references 'supporting reference files' alongside each SKILL.md but never defines what those files are, how many there are, or what they should contain. An executing agent cannot know what to create without this specification.
findings.location: ## What — 'each a `SKILL.md` plus supporting reference files'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: 'Each skill directory contains a `SKILL.md` (the main prompt/instruction file) and, where applicable, a `REFERENCE.md` listing key rebar commands or links. If no supplementary material is needed, only `SKILL.md` is required.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for AC2, e.g. a grep/ripgrep one-liner that fails if absolute paths or common secret patterns are found, so the criterion is mechanically verifiable.
findings.finding: AC2 ('No personal/user/host/secret/absolute-path leakage in committed content') has no associated proving command or scan recipe. The claim is security-relevant but is left as an implicit inspection-only check with no specified tool or pattern.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — 'No personal / user / host / secret / absolute-path leakage in committed content.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2: 'Verify with: `grep -rE "(/home/|/Users/|/root/|[A-Z]:\\\\|secret|password|api.?key)" examples/claude-skills/ && echo FAIL || echo PASS`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC with a concrete validation mechanism for contributors — e.g., 'A reviewer or contributor follows each SKILL.md end-to-end in a test repo and confirms the workflow produces the intended rebar artifact' or equivalent dogfooding step.
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion carries a concrete validation mechanism showing the skills actually help a contributor accomplish an agentic workflow — all ACs are structural (dirs exist), negative (no leakage), or process (Gerrit merge) checks, leaving value to the named beneficiary (contributors) unvalidated.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'Each SKILL.md has been exercised in a representative repo by at least one reviewer, confirming the described workflow produces the expected rebar output (plan, log entry, or passing CI).' This closes the gap between structural existence and demonstrated contributor value.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the 'supporting reference files' for each skill directory (e.g., list expected filenames like `COMMANDS.md`, `WORKFLOW.md`, etc.) so the complete file set is auditable against the no-leakage and completeness ACs.
findings.finding: The plan references 'supporting reference files' alongside each SKILL.md but does not enumerate what those files are, making the complete set of files-to-be-created unknowable from the plan. This means scope accuracy cannot be confirmed and the no-personal/secret-leakage AC (#2) cannot be audited against a known file list.
findings.location: ## What — 'each a SKILL.md plus supporting reference files'
findings.suggested_fix: In the ## What section, replace 'each a SKILL.md plus supporting reference files' with a concrete per-skill file list, e.g.: 'each directory contains a `SKILL.md` (the skill definition) and zero or more named reference files (e.g. `COMMANDS.md`, `EXAMPLES.md`); the full list is: [enumerate them]'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sentence to the 'Why' (or a new 'Approach' note) explaining the positive rationale for using per-skill directories under `examples/claude-skills/` — e.g., that it keeps illustrative material clearly separate from authoritative project docs, follows Claude Code's per-skill directory convention, and allows each skill its own supporting reference files.
findings.finding: The plan's 'Why' section gives user-value rationale (show contributors one way agents can use rebar) but omits a positive rationale for the chosen structural approach — specifically why four separate SKILL.md directories under `examples/claude-skills/` rather than, say, a single prose document in `docs/` or content integrated alongside `CLAUDE.md`. Per G6, the absence of a positive approach rationale is a (minor) required element.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Why or Scope section, add: 'A dedicated `examples/claude-skills/` tree (rather than embedding in `docs/`) keeps illustrative, non-authoritative content clearly separate from project-operational docs; the per-skill directory structure mirrors Claude Code's own skill convention and lets each skill carry its own supporting reference files independently.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define what 'supporting reference files' means for each skill (e.g., enumerate expected filenames, note 'only SKILL.md is required if no supplementary material is needed,' or name a template) so an executor can act without guessing.
findings.finding: The plan references 'supporting reference files' alongside each SKILL.md but never defines the vocabulary for this artifact type: what files are expected, how many, what names they carry, or what content they must contain. An agent or human executor writing skill directories has no schema to follow.
findings.location: ## What — 'each a `SKILL.md` plus supporting reference files'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: 'Each skill directory contains a `SKILL.md` (the main prompt/instruction file) and, where needed, a `REFERENCE.md` listing key rebar commands or links; if no supplementary material is needed, only `SKILL.md` is required.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief per-skill description in the What section stating what workflow each SKILL.md covers (e.g., what steps, what rebar operations, what outputs the skill guides an agent through), so the content vocabulary is defined and executable.
findings.finding: The four skill names (rebar-brainstorm, rebar-debug, rebar-implement, rebar-janitor) are referenced as a defined set but no description of what workflow each skill encodes is provided. An executor writing the SKILL.md files has no processing protocol or decision-rule vocabulary co-located with the instruction to create them.
findings.location: ## What — skill directory names; no per-skill descriptions in plan body

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Move the rebar-implement content requirement (review-and-land steps deferring to project docs) from AC#3 into the What section's description of rebar-implement, so the content constraint is co-located with the artifact description.
findings.finding: AC#3 encodes a content requirement for rebar-implement ('review-and-land steps defer to project docs') that is stated only in the Acceptance Criteria and never described in the plan body. This is an instruction-locality antipattern: an executor writing the SKILL.md reads the What/Scope sections, not the ACs, to understand content requirements.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC#3 vs. ## What section
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, expand the rebar-implement entry to: 'rebar-implement — guides an agent through planning, coding, and landing a change; its review-and-land steps explicitly defer to the project's own docs (rebar plan + Gerrit workflow) rather than prescribing a fixed process.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan (or delegate explicitly to the per-skill descriptions) whether each SKILL.md must include fallback/error-path guidance for failed sub-steps, or explicitly acknowledge that happy-path-only is acceptable for illustrative examples.
findings.finding: No fallback or error-path behavior is specified for any of the four skills. The plan describes these as 'agentic development workflows' but provides no guidance on what an agent following a skill should do when a sub-step fails or is incomplete (e.g., rebar-debug when the bug cannot be reproduced, rebar-implement when CI is red).
findings.location: ## What; ## Scope — 'agentic development workflows with rebar'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
